summary: My father was born in France, which was a significant stop for my family after leaving Poland. My grandfather initially moved there because he thought France was more enlightened than Eastern Europe for Jews. However, before World War II, he realized France was not as safe as he hoped, prompting the family to move further away to Australia.
cues: grandfather moved before World War II

summary: My family origins trace back to Poland, where my grandfather was born. He was a visionary man who left Poland because he felt Eastern Europe wasn't safe or suitable for Jews. This decision set the course for our family's migration first to France and then to Australia before the war.
cues: migration due to Eastern Europe conditions

summary: Australia became my family's home after my grandfather decided it was the safest place farthest from Europe. My father's side settled there, and I grew up in Melbourne. My parents owned a steak restaurant there, and I started my culinary journey in Australia before making Aliyah to Israel in 1984.
cues: family moved to Australia before World War II

summary: On my mother's side, our family history is quite different; we discovered that her paternal ancestors came to Australia as convicts from England in the 1830s. This was a surprising revelation since we had thought they came freely. So, England is part of my heritage through that convict lineage.

I grew up in Melbourne, Australia, where my family owned a steak restaurant called the Barbecue Inn. It was a popular place serving Australian beef and some Central European dishes, thanks to a Yugoslavian pastry chef we employed. Melbourne also had a strong Jewish community, including Landsmanshaftn, which connected my family to their Polish roots.

At my father's restaurant in Melbourne, we had a Yugoslavian pastry chef who taught us many Central European dishes. This was before Yugoslavia fell apart, and she introduced us to foods like cevapcici, a Balkan kabob full of garlic. Her influence was part of my early exposure to diverse cuisines.

Cevapcici was one of the Central European dishes I learned about through the Yugoslavian pastry chef at my father's restaurant in Melbourne. It's a Balkan-style kabob, rich in garlic, and it was part of the diverse culinary influences I was exposed to growing up. This dish helped shape my appreciation for regional cuisines.

My father was quite the food adventurer and would make dishes like Peking duck at home, even hanging the duck up to dry it out properly. This was during the 1970s when I was growing up, and such experiences fueled my passion for food and cooking. It was part of the diverse culinary environment I was raised in.

Alongside other dishes like Peking duck, my father also made wontons from scratch, which was part of his adventurous approach to food. These experiences in my childhood exposed me to a wide range of culinary techniques and flavors. It was a formative part of my journey to becoming a chef and food expert.

I made Aliyah to Israel in 1984 when I was 25 years old, after several visits starting with a gap year in 1978. I was drawn by a strong Jewish identity and Zionist youth movements like Habonim and Hashomer Hatzair. I settled in the Western Galilee, living in the community neighborhood of Tuval, and have since become recognized as an expert in Galilean cuisine.

Living in the Western Galilee has deeply influenced my culinary work; I specialize in Galilean cuisine, which includes local Arabic dishes and more. We lived on Tuval, a community in the mountains of the Western Galilee, which I believe is one of the best places in the world to raise children. The region's culture and food are inseparable in my life and work.

When I made my way to Israel in 1984, I traveled overland and flew to Bali from Northern Australia. Bali was a key stop on my journey across Asia before continuing through India and eventually reaching Italy. This trip was part of my broader experience exploring cultures and food, which has always been central to my life.

During my 1984 journey to Israel, I traveled overland through India after Bali. I wanted to continue into Pakistan and Afghanistan but found it too dangerous at the time. This overland trip was part of my broader experience of exploring cultures and cuisines before settling in Israel.

I had intended to travel through Pakistan on my way to Israel in 1984, but it was too dangerous at the time, so I had to alter my route. This was part of my adventurous journey from Australia, through Asia, before finally arriving in Israel. The experience of traveling through these regions enriched my understanding of culture and food.

Like Pakistan, Afghanistan was on my intended route during my 1984 journey to Israel, but it was too unsafe to enter then. I had to reroute through Italy instead. This journey was a formative experience that combined my love for travel, culture, and food before settling in Israel.

After traveling through Asia, I flew into Italy as part of my overland journey to Israel in 1984. Italy was a key transit point before I finally made Aliyah. This trip was not just about getting to Israel but also about immersing myself in different cultures and cuisines along the way.

I was involved in the Jewish youth movement Habonim Dror during my early visits to Israel, starting with a gap year in 1978. This movement was part of my strong Jewish identity and connection to Zionism. It helped shape my commitment to Israel and influenced my decision to make Aliyah.

After Habonim, I became involved with Hashomer Hatzair, a more left-wing Jewish youth movement known as the Young Guard. I met my wife Ilya through this group in 1984, which was a pivotal moment in my life. This involvement deepened my connection to Israel and Zionism.

While my parents were not particularly Zionist, I developed a strong Jewish identity and Zionist fervor through my involvement in youth movements like Habonim and Hashomer Hatzair. This passion motivated me to make Aliyah in 1984 and commit to building a life in Israel. Zionism has been a significant part of my personal and communal identity.

I attended Mount Scopus Memorial College in Melbourne, the largest Jewish day school in the diaspora. This school was a central part of my Jewish upbringing and helped foster my strong Jewish identity. It connected me to the broader Jewish community and culture before I moved to Israel.

In Melbourne during the 1970s, there were still Landsmanshaftn, which were micro-communities of Polish Jews. My grandfather was involved with the Warsaw Landsmanshaftn, which kept our Polish Jewish heritage alive. These groups helped maintain a strong sense of community and identity among immigrants like my family.

Tuval is the community in the Western Galilee where my family lived after leaving the kibbutz. We chose to live there because it offered an ideal environment to raise our four children. Living in Tuval has been central to our family life and my work in Galilean cuisine.

entity: Scud missile attacks during the Gulf War
summary: My firstborn son Gael was born in January 1991, right during the Gulf War when Scud missile attacks were happening. He spent his second night of life in a gas-proof tent due to the threat. It was a surreal experience, but we just accepted it as part of life in Israel at that time.

The Oslo Accords were unfolding around the early 1990s when my children were young. This period was full of hope and tension in Israel, coinciding with my family's early years here. It was a significant historical backdrop to our lives as we settled in the country.
